incidents.description: Target agreed to a $4.6 million settlement resolving a class action alleging it failed to compensate warehouse workers at three New Jersey distribution centers for mandatory pre-shift and post-shift security screening time and walking time to and from workstations.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey

incidents.description: The EEOC found reasonable cause that three pre-hire assessments used by Target for exempt-level professional positions had a disparate impact on applicants based on race, sex, and disability, and Target agreed to pay $2.8 million and discontinue use of the assessments.
Target agreed to settle a class action alleging it failed to disclose wage scales and salary ranges in Washington job postings as required by state wage transparency law.
Target agreed to pay $1.25 million to resolve a proposed class action alleging it failed to provide non-exempt employees with proper meal and rest breaks as required by applicable wage and hour laws.

The EEOC reached a settlement requiring Target to pay $775,000 to resolve racial harassment allegations affecting a class of 14 African-American employees, with Target also required to implement anti-harassment training and policies.

Target agreed to pay $510,000 via consent decree to resolve EEOC allegations that it discriminated against four African-American applicants for Entry Team Leader positions in District 110 based on race.

Target agreed to pay $160,000 to settle an EEOC lawsuit alleging it failed to provide a reasonable accommodation for a cart attendant with cerebral palsy at its Foothill Ranch store in Orange County, California.
Target agreed to pay $45,000 to settle an EEOC lawsuit alleging it denied a reasonable accommodation to an applicant or employee with a disability.
Target was assessed an $11,269 penalty for a labor relations violation recorded by the National Labor Relations Board.

A class action was filed against Target alleging it failed to compensate hourly warehouse workers at Wilton and Amsterdam, New York facilities for time spent walking to and from assigned work departments before clocking in and after clocking out.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of New York

incidents.description: An unfair labor practice case filed against Target in Bozeman, Montana (NLRB Case No. 19-CA-341076) was closed following a unilateral settlement agreement approved by the NLRB General Counsel.

Target reached a settlement with the Communications Workers of America and two union members resolving allegations that Target's social media job advertisements used age-targeting features that excluded older workers in violation of the Age Discrimination in Employment Act.

An unfair labor practice case filed against Target in Christiansburg, Virginia (NLRB Case No. 10-CA-256402), involving allegations of unlawful changes to terms and conditions of employment and retaliatory discharge, was closed following withdrawal approval.

The California Attorney General reached a settlement with Target Corporation requiring the company to pay $22.5 million in penalties, attorneys' fees, and supplemental environmental project funding to resolve allegations of illegal dumping of hazardous waste at multiple California facilities.

California Attorney General Xavier Becerra and 24 local government attorneys announced a $7.4 million statewide settlement with Target Corporation resolving allegations that Target improperly disposed of hazardous waste—including electronics, batteries, aerosol cans, pharmaceuticals, and medical waste—into landfills between 2012 and 2016, in violation of state laws and injunctive terms from a prior 2011 judgment.
incidents.agency_or_court: California Attorney General / Alameda County Superior Court

incidents.description: The U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission announced that Target agreed to pay a $600,000 civil penalty for knowingly importing and selling toys with paint containing lead levels above the legal limit between May 2006 and August 2007, in violation of the federal lead paint ban.

The California Air Resources Board settled with Target for selling RainX 2-in-1 All Season and Splash Windshield Wash products in California that exceeded the 1 percent by weight VOC limit for automotive windshield washer fluids under California Consumer Products Regulations.

EPA issued a Consent Agreement and Final Order requiring Target to pay a $120,000 civil penalty for distributing and selling 'Horrible Spooky String' novelty products containing banned hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in violation of Section 610 of the Clean Air Act.

Target entered a prospective purchaser agreement with the EPA, paying $100,000 for liability protection under CERCLA related to pre-existing contamination at the Denova Environmental Superfund Site in Rialto, California, which Target was redeveloping as a distribution center.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency / U.S. Department of Justice

incidents.description: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) reached a settlement with Target Corporation for selling uncertified indoor air cleaning devices in California without required CARB certification and for failing to display mandatory advisories on its website, in violation of the Regulation for Limiting Ozone Emissions From Indoor Air Cleaning Devices.

Target Brands, Inc. and Target Corporation were named defendants in a Proposition 65 enforcement action filed by the Environmental Research Center, Inc. alleging failure to warn consumers of lead and mercury exposure in dietary supplement products; the case resulted in a settlement including a permanent injunction against a co-defendant.

The California District Attorney's office issued a 60-Day Notice of Intent to Sue against Target Brands, Inc. and Target Corporation for alleged failure to provide required Proposition 65 warnings regarding cadmium exposure from Target's Good & Gather Cut Leaf Spinach product.
incidents.agency_or_court: California District Attorney / California Health & Safety Code § 25249.5

incidents.description: EPA Region 10 issued a Stop Sale, Use, or Removal Order (SSURO) to Target Corporation prohibiting distribution and sale of misbranded 'Hoover Steam Mop Cleaner' devices found to violate FIFRA Section 12(a)(1)(F) due to false and misleading pesticide claims on product labels.

A class action lawsuit was filed against Target in the U.S. District Court for the District of Minnesota alleging that Target's 'Target Clean' label misled consumers by marketing beauty products as clean and free from unwanted chemicals when the products allegedly contained harmful or unwanted ingredients.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota

incidents.description: Target settled a yearslong antitrust dispute with Visa accusing Visa of participating in an illegal anticompetitive scheme that forced merchants to pay excessive fees on credit and debit card transactions.

Target Corporation received a total of $189.2 million in state and local subsidies across 224 separate awards, with no federal grants or allocated tax credits, according to Good Jobs First Subsidy Tracker data.
Target Corporation received $29.32 million across 48 awards from states other than New York, Illinois, Tennessee, Iowa, and North Carolina.

Target agreed to pay $18.5 million to settle investigations by 47 state attorneys general and the District of Columbia related to the 2013 holiday-season data breach affecting over 40 million payment card accounts and 60 million customers.
incidents.agency_or_court: Multistate Attorneys General (47 states and D.C.)

incidents.description: Target settled class action lawsuits alleging it deceptively marketed its RedCard store debit cards, including misrepresenting how transactions were processed and exposing consumers to unrestricted returned payment fees.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California (3:16-cv-01678); U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota (0:18-cv-02660)

incidents.description: Target settled a class action brought by the National Federation of the Blind alleging that Target.com was inaccessible to blind consumers in violation of the ADA and California civil rights statutes, paying $6 million to the California settlement class.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court (National Federation of the Blind v. Target Corporation)

incidents.description: A class of current and former Target warehouse workers settled state law claims that they were not compensated for time spent walking to and from mandatory pre- and post-shift security screenings at distribution centers.

Target settled allegations that its criminal background check policy discriminated against African-American and Latino job applicants, brought by the NAACP Legal Defense Fund in the Southern District of New York.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York

incidents.description: The EEOC found that Target's pre-hire employment assessments disproportionately screened out applicants based on race and sex in violation of Title VII, and that one assessment violated the ADA, resulting in a $2.8 million settlement.
Target agreed to settle a class action alleging it failed to disclose wage scales and salary ranges in job postings as required by Washington state law between January 1, 2023, and July 26, 2025.
incidents.agency_or_court: Washington Superior Court, King County (Case No. 24-2-25091-3-SEA)

incidents.description: Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison announced a settlement with Shipt, Inc., resolving a 2022 lawsuit alleging Shipt misclassified delivery workers as independent contractors, depriving them of minimum wage, overtime, and other employment protections.

The EPA announced that Target would pay a civil penalty for distributing and selling Horrible Spooky String, a children's product containing banned hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in violation of the Clean Air Act.
OSHA issued a citation to Target for a repeat workplace safety violation with a penalty of $57,830 for failure to abate a prior inspection violation.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration

incidents.description: The California Air Resources Board settled with Target for selling indoor air cleaning devices in California without required CARB certification, in violation of the Regulation for Limiting Ozone Emissions From Indoor Air Cleaning Devices.

The FDA issued a warning letter to Target for failing to remove recalled ByHeart Whole Nutrition Infant Formula from store shelves across 20 states during a multistate infant botulism outbreak linked to the product.

An NLRB unfair labor practice case (05-CA-366130) filed in Towson, Maryland in May 2025, alleging 8(a)(1) violations including retaliation, discharge, and discipline, was closed after a withdrawal request was approved.

A securities lawsuit alleging Target misled investors by failing to disclose risks of customer backlash to its 2023 Pride Month marketing campaign survived a motion to dismiss, with the court finding the complaint adequately alleged falsity and scienter.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Florida

incidents.description: An NLRB unfair labor practice case (19-CA-341076) filed in Bozeman, Montana in April 2024 was closed following a unilateral settlement agreement approved by the NLRB General Counsel.

per_category.overflow_note: Public records document 2 additional institutional enablement actions between 2007 and 2025. Major incidents shown above.

incidents.description: Target settled a multi-state enforcement action over a 2013 data breach that compromised payment card information of over 40 million customers, with allegations that Target failed to provide reasonable data security.
Target agreed to a $10 million class action settlement in federal Multi-District Litigation over the 2013 data breach that allegedly compromised personal information of nearly 100 million consumers.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota

incidents.description: Target debit card holders reached a class action settlement over claims that Target deceptively marketed its RedCard store debit card as functioning like a traditional debit card when it operated over the ACH network with delayed processing.

Target settled a $7.4 million statewide enforcement action in California resolving allegations of improper hazardous waste disposal into landfills between 2012 and 2016, in violation of state laws and terms of a 2011 stipulated judgment.
incidents.agency_or_court: California Attorney General / Alameda County Superior Court

incidents.description: Target settled a New Jersey class action lawsuit alleging it failed to compensate warehouse workers for time spent undergoing mandatory pre- and post-shift security screenings and walking to and from workstations.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey

incidents.description: Target settled a class action lawsuit alleging it failed to disclose wage scales and salary ranges in Washington state job postings as required by state law.

The EPA assessed Target Corporation a $120,000 civil penalty for distributing and selling Horrible Spooky String, a children's product containing banned hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in violation of the Clean Air Act.
Target Corporation settled an EEOC disability discrimination lawsuit for failing to provide reasonable accommodations as required under the Americans with Disabilities Act.
The NLRB issued a citation against Target Corporation for a labor relations violation, resulting in a penalty of $11,269.

A proposed class action was filed alleging that Target and its subsidiary Shipt deceived California consumers by advertising free or flat-rate delivery but charging an undisclosed $3.99 'CA Shoppers Benefits Fee' at checkout.

A complaint was filed with the NLRB against Target's Bozeman, Montana location regarding an alleged labor relations violation; the case was closed via a Unilateral Settlement Agreement on October 8, 2024.

Attorneys general for Minnesota and Washington D.C. filed separate lawsuits against Target-owned Shipt alleging the company misclassified delivery workers as independent contractors to avoid labor costs, blocking workers from minimum wage, overtime, sick leave, unemployment insurance, and workers' compensation protections.
incidents.agency_or_court: Minnesota Attorney General / Washington D.C. Attorney General

incidents.description: UN human rights special rapporteurs communicated to Target Corporation expressing grave concerns about the company's potential supply chain involvement in alleged forced labor, arbitrary detention, and trafficking of Uyghur and other minority workers in China's Xinjiang region.
incidents.agency_or_court: UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights

incidents.description: Target Australia ceased sourcing cotton from China's Xinjiang province following reported evidence that Uyghur Muslims were being forced to work in textile factories in the region.

Target faced allegations of underage labor practices at a supplier of national brand bread products sold at Target, prompting Target to launch an independent investigation into hiring practices at the vendor and any third-party temporary agencies involved.

per_category.overflow_note: Public records document 2 additional supply chain actions between 2007 and 2024. Major incidents shown above.

known_subsidiaries: Target Sourcing Services India Pvt Ltd

gaps.note: No incidents reported in this period. Gap may reflect absence of major enforcement actions or litigation filed against Target during these years, or incomplete source coverage between the 2008 ADA settlement and 2012–2015 data breach / wage enforcement cluster.
No incidents pre-date 2007. This likely reflects the limits of available digital source coverage rather than an absence of misconduct.

No incidents recorded in this six-year window despite Target's large workforce; possible underreporting, settlements without public disclosure, or absence of major enforcement actions during this period.

Only one incident (NLRB Virginia ULP, 2020) adjacent to this range; gap likely reflects lack of available public enforcement data rather than absence of misconduct, particularly given the high volume of incidents before and after.

gaps.period: Two undated incidents (California disability, national disability)
gaps.note: Two EEOC incidents lack date fields entirely, preventing chronological placement and trend analysis. Original sources should be consulted to establish filing or resolution years.

No discrete enforcement incidents recorded during this period despite the 2018 settlement explicitly covering misconduct from 2012–2016; underlying violations were only surfaced through the 2018 action and no stand-alone cases appear in the dataset.
No incidents recorded in the dataset for this four-year window; possible underreporting or absence of major enforcement actions during this period.
No subsidy records provided for early corporate history; Good Jobs First tracking coverage is stronger for more recent decades
No federal grant or allocated tax credit incidents recorded; may reflect genuine absence or incomplete federal-level tracking

net_public_transfer.math_note: Total subsidies of $189.2 million documented across 224 state and local awards per Good Jobs First Subsidy Tracker. No penalty amounts are present in the incident data, so net transfer cannot be calculated. Note: the $29.32 million figure across 48 awards appears to be a subset of the $189.2 million total and is not additive.

summaries.one_line: Target Corporation is a major U.S. retail chain with documented public subsidy receipts across multiple states.
summaries.worker_summary: No labor, wage, discrimination, or union-related incidents are present in the available incident data for Target Corporation. The documented record reflects only public subsidy awards and does not include findings related to workforce practices.
summaries.community_summary: No environmental, product safety, or civil rights incidents are present in the available incident data for Target Corporation. The documented record is limited to public subsidy receipts and does not reflect findings in these categories.
summaries.public_transfer_note: Target Corporation received at least $189.2 million in documented state and local subsidies across 224 awards; net public transfer cannot be calculated as no corresponding penalty amounts are present in the available data.
